Its origin is obscure - partly pagan, partly emergent through centuries of practice and survival - but the principle is clear: transformation comes through struggle, risk, and personal engagement. Theban Sorcery, in contrast, is structured, hierarchical, and rigid: angels, mandates, rules, divine judgment. For Ivana, Crúac feels alive and intuitive, which is reflective of how she currently exists - and how she always has. It’s messy, evolving, and reactive, which feels natural to her, while Theban approaches seem artificial, distant, and disconnected from reality. Also, they stole it from us, so screw them.
answers.q09: Circle rituals aren’t set in stone - they’re constantly evolving, reflective of the Circle's philosophy. Every ceremony mixes old and new, shifts depending on place and people, and new rites are always being created to keep the faith alive. Ritual isn’t just “doing the same thing over and over”; it’s a living practice, a way to respond to the world and the spirits in it. In a sense, ritual is how the Circle interacts with the ecosystem of the world - a direct expression of their naturalist, change-embracing philosophy. This means faith is intensely personal and practical, and there is no wrong way for you to practice your faith and show your devotion. There is no inherent or expected "perfect" or "right" way to do things - it's just a form of adaptability, a tool for growth and survival, and a way to engage with the world and the Beast in a meaningul way.
answers.q10: Mortal cults and secret societies are woven into practice. They’re not just followers; they can be chorus, ritual participants, recruits, or even sacrificial components. Acolytes see mortals as part of the ecosystem of power, devotion, and magic. They lead, guide, and integrate mortals pragmatically. Relationships vary - some are protective, some instrumental, some experimental - but the common thread is that mortals are part of the ritual and the community, not just furniture, or background scenery.
answers.q11: New members don’t just get handed a welcome pamphlet, patted on the butt and sent on their way. They go through a kind of trial period called the Chorus. The Circle isn’t impressed by curiosity or flair; it values endurance, adaptability, and devotion proven through action. Sacrifice, creativity, willingness to face messy truths, surviving discomfort - that’s what counts. An Acolyte sizing up potential recruits might look out for people who can survive pressure, act without needing pomp and ceremony, participation ribbons or applause, and handle change without whining. Anyone drawn just by blood, darkness, or ritual-as-fashion? Instant skepticism. As I've said before, I think (I've been answering these questions over days) - actions speak louder than words, prove yourself through action, put your money where your mouth is.
answers.q12: Circle-led domains are chaotic, flexible, and guided by “Natural Law,” not hierarchy. Leadership favors those who survive, work magic, and prove themselves. Domains can become lush, vibrant spaces or bloody, ruthless realms depending on omens and who’s calling the shots. Mortals may participate as ritualists, recruits, or sacrifices. Rivals like the Lance are sometimes tolerated, sometimes hunted; alliances with other covenants are transactional and strategic, not spiritual.
answers.q13: True Acolytes are shaped by Crúac, fully surrendering to the Beast, the gods, and the covenant’s way of unliving. Dabblers treat magic like a recipe; genuine followers live it, endure it, and accept that their soul will bear the marks of ritual, trial, and sacrifice. Devotion is visible in willingness to face brutal rites without hesitation because real commitment comes at a cost. Being an Acolyte is not just performative, like Happy Clappy Sundays, as I mentioned, it's how you live, and how you show up in the broader ecosystem of the Circle. Acolytes embrace the “bliss of monstrosity,” living naturally as predators without hiding behind human morals or the dogma of other covenants.
answers.q14: Because the Circle teaches that suffering refines and tribulation brings enlightenment (which make sense on paper), it is a real dangerous thing for her. When the Circle says “pain teaches,” she already knows that firsthand. That makes her vulnerable to taking the doctrine too literally - tolerating abuse, pushing herself into harm, or seeing suffering as inherently sacred rather than a tool to navigate the world. Her past makes her susceptible to overextending herself in the name of growth, misreading cruelty as instruction, or confusing endurance with virtue. Given that she is looking to reinvent herself and find herself, she'll need to be careful and learn to take what the Circle offers without letting her history blind her to what’s real danger vs what’s meaningful trial.
answers.q15: For Ivana, reverence tips into savagery the moment ritual stops listening and starts proving. When meaning gets replaced by momentum, violence becomes habit, indulgence, or expression rather than offering or invocation. A Crúac rite with bloodletting? Sure - that’s sacred. But when participants escalate harm for ego, thrill, or emotional release, she sees the line crossed. She’s hyper-aware of this because of her past - she’s survived brutality without reason and brutality for ego, so she instinctively reads the difference between discipline and chaos. Brutality that serves transformation or invocation she accepts; brutality that serves spectacle or ego she distrusts. In play, she would quietly observe, noting whether a ritual still has purpose and intent, stepping in only if the sacred spine is lost.
answers.q16: Again, aiming for a reprieve for you on this one. Other covenants keep coming back to the Circle because, for all their fear, they crave what the Circle doesn’t hide - the raw truth of what it means to be Kindred. Gonna pick on the Ordo here - they desperately want evolution, insight, and mastery over their curse, but without ever having to surrender to the Beast inside. They want power, transcendence, and mystical perception, yet they can’t admit the one thing that actually unlocks it - total reckoning with their own monstrosity. They keep coming back because the Circle doesn’t sugarcoat that price. The Acolytes show that transformation isn’t neat or controlled - it’s messy, dangerous, and requires letting the primal part of yourself lead at times.
answers.q17: As I've said before, the Circle describes Ivana's life - she fits the Circle because its principles echo her lived experience. She isn’t looking for absolution, hierarchy, or transcendence. She’s looking for context: a framework where her Embrace isn’t a mistake, survival isn’t shameful, power can grow from damage, and monstrosity has meaning. Something that will help me find herself and her identity, after a lifetime of not having one. Ivana was always unwanted, bad, not good enough, trash, a passing toy, wrong, stupid etc. Even in death, she felt that way, but her innate connection to Crúac and all things that have happened before and since mean that the views of the Circle are likely the only views of all the Covenants that don't reinforce the messages she was given in life... So it's a nice natural fit. She didn't choose the Crone lyf, Crone lyf chose her.
answers.q18: Ivana’s practice isn’t formalised yet. She reads the city itself and its dark corners, thresholds, and hidden currents, as living, meaningful systems. She doesn’t worship a goddess by name; she listens to patterns and responses in her environment. Ritual and spirituality are about place, survival, and presence, not ceremony for its own sake. Her sewer-born origin and urban attunement make this approach organic, responsive, and grounded rather than doctrinal. So, she'll learn a lot more about those specific things as she spends more time with more Crone, but looking at how all of that gels with some of the things mentioned in the book, the "Nun in Shadows" path jumps out, as does the "maiden seeker" thing. This is honestly a question that I am the most unsure of my answer to, so guidance is appreciated.
answers.q19: Another one I'm pretty unsure about, but I've had a crack. Ivana doesn’t really have direct relationships with other Acolytes yet, as far as she's aware, save for Keeper, but he hasn't done much to mentor her, as far as I can tell. She believes the darkness talks to her, and in a way, that’s true - first, in the form of Charlie in the sewers, helping her from the shadows, and now through an elder mentor (per my sheet, likely connected to her via Charlie also helping guide her from the shadow, letting her interpret the city and its hidden corners as a living, meaningful system. So yeah, Ivana may never know that she is being mentored - or maybe she does? But, she still talks to the darkness, and the city, like it's her guardian angel.
answers.q20: Ivana doesn’t openly rebel - making a scene isn’t her style, and it's a quick way to get yourself killed. She shifts her practice into quiet, hidden corners, small gestures, or symbolic acts that no one would notice. Survival comes first, visibility comes later - if at all. She still listens to the city - the tunnels, forgotten thresholds, the patterns of shadow and movement - and treats those as her ritual space. The restriction doesn’t stop her; it just changes how she practices. To her, it’s not about defiance, it’s about keeping the work alive while avoiding unnecessary attention.
answers.q21: Genuinely not sure, really. I expect that this response would likely change depending on the when of it all, and/or what mood she may be in that evening. But, if this were to occur at the first Court, she would probably not argue. She would either ignore and retreat into a corner, or behind the Wall of Charlie, or the other Crone. If she was feeling a little comfy, or sassy or competitive, she might come back with a quiet, but grounded, "Your way is your way, my way is my way." She doesn't care about approval (in this context - unless it's Charlie) or winning a debate - it’s about what’s real in her experience. The priest’s outrage might sting a bit, but it'd honestly be the least impactful outburst she's likely ever faced, and so is unlikely to rattle her tooooo much. Again, depends on her mood. But, whatever happens, she'd wander away content that she knows the darkness, and the city have more to teach her than any sermon ever could.
answers.q22:
Ivana treats the mortal as a problem to solve, not a moral question. Assess first - are they scared? Curious? Dangerous? Can she guide them or contain them quietly? Source memory control if necessary, relocation if possible, or controlled exposure if they seem compatible with the energy of the site. She would likely only consider harm if the ritual literally requires it. Her experience with exploitation makes her prefers careful handling over thoughtless violence. For her, it’s about balancing safety, secrecy, and the integrity of the work.

When in doubt, get a more adultier adult, however.
answers.q23: Ivana wouldn’t panic or call for punishment, but she knows refusing a core Circle practice is serious. She’d watch first, assessing whether the refusal comes from fear, confusion, or ideology. If it’s uncertainty, she’d expect the Circle to teach through trials or Crucibles, maybe even forcing participation. If it’s stubborn ideology, she sees the person as unready and lets the covenant handle the consequences, observing pragmatically rather than judging.

answers.q02: Creation through conflict. Monsters made, not born. Before beauty comes intense suffering. Pain for power. [This connects with Jack's back story:  he was groomed, tormented, driven to the pit of despair, and then Embraced to become something horrible.] Kindred must continue to suffer for their souls to evolve; comfort leads to stagnation.  Reject your humanity; be a perfect monster instead.     The spiritual focus is Cruac and Blood. It is pagan, visceral and personal, requires sacrifice, ecstatic emotions and Kindred are part of a natural (not unnatural) cycle with Embrace essentially a rebirth.  They are the opposite of Lancea.
answers.q03: Vampiricism is holy evolution, neither curse nor blessing. The Embrace is a second birth, value created from agony. The Lancean idea that vampires are the Curse of Caine is idiotic, insulting. Vampiricism is not about guilt or punishment, nor are Kindred victims. The Beast is not the Devil or a lapse of control, it is a sacred engine to be harnessed, the primal energy of the Mother of Monsters.  By leaning into our predatory nature, we get closer to the primal truth of the universe.  Humans are seeds, planted by the Embrace, and we grow through the Requiem.  Trying to go back to our humanity is a step backwards.
answers.q04: There are many cults under banner of the Mother. We have no Church or centralised structure, like the Lancea; instead, we have a unifying language -- Cruac. Blood sorcery connects us, makes us "of the blood", regardless of variations in our practices.  The unified theology has three pillars:  creation through conflict or pain makes us stronger; the Divine Mother is the source -- she is feminine, fertile and absolutely terrifying (Queen of the Dead vibes); and Vitae is Sacred, not just food.
answers.q05: Crone's value "The Molt" -- kindred must constantly shed their skin to evolve and improve. Destructive change (such as upheaval or crisis) forces vampires to adapt.  The situation in Sydney is a perfect example of the Mother's actions to provide Kindred with an opportunity to evolve.  This is different from the Invictus, which is threatened by change; and the Lancea, which embraces change only through its religion; and Carthians, which are really just simpletons that only understand political or social change, not spiritual.
answers.q06: The Beast is a sacred predator, the source of our power and catalyst for transformation, and in so doing, increase in power and evolution. The Beast is the most honest truth about a vampire's nature as a predator, it cannot be denied. To some Acolytes, the Beast is the spirit of the Mother herself, who resides in all Kindred. The Beast must be embraced to be mastered.     In contrast, the Invictus see the Beast as a liability to order and the Masquerade; the Lanceans see the Beast as divine punishment; and Carthians see the Beast as a personal matter that shouldn't interfere with Kindred society.
answers.q07: Circle sees the First Estate as arrogant parents trying to impose artificial laws on a primal world. THat said, the Invictus does provide order to allow the Circle to practice safely.  The Carthians' obsession with human politics and rights is ridiculous and naive. Vampires are godlike predators --- democracy is irrelevant. That said, we can ally with Carthians if they serve to oppose a common enemy, and chaos of revolution can facilitate The Molt.  The relationship with the Lanceans is a theological war, well beyond disagreement.  While the Lance views Crones as heretics; we see them as slaves to a human religion.  This conflict is reflected in our magic (Cruac vs Theban sorcery) -- of which Cruac is superior!
answers.q08: Cruac is blood sorcery, a power created and granted to Acolytes by the Crone. It harnesses the power of the Beast by channeling vitae through ecstatic rituals that embrace our primal connection and monstrous nature. In contrast Theban Sorcery is (supposedly...) power granted or cursed by God through prayer and rites. Cruac also erodes humanity while theban sorcery requires humanity.
answers.q09: Circle rituals draw from local traditions (scandinavian, voodoo, aztec, etc.) and adapt to modern settings by incorporating elements from modern times into rituals. Acolytes embrace change and will adapt as times and settings change. "life is change" is a core teaching of the Mother, requiring Acolytes to embrace change or face stagnation.  This opens up endless variations for Cruac rituals. Because of this variation, there is no central leadership and Crones can practice as cults/broods that are unique. The 'Cult of One' is possible in the Cirlce -- not in the Lancea.
answers.q10: Mortal cults serve many functions: a source of worship to reinforce an Acolyte's connection to the old gods or Mother; or to echo their own experience of hardship to see who is worthy; they provide a herd, protection and source of materials for rituals. Acolytes can relate to them in different ways -- maternal, inspiring divine awe or as cultivators/experimenters. Again, lots of variety.
answers.q11: Members are brought into the Circle through a rite of passage -- they are reborn. They have been observed and tested, broken through ordeal and then brought ceremoniously into the fold. This involves consuming the vitae of an elder Crone (title of the blood) and participate in their first Cruac ritual to symbolise the connection to the Mother.
answers.q12: Praxis held by a Crone is very different from other covenants. The domain becomes a holy wilderness of ecstasy and terror where the laws of the blood apply -- the Masquerade isn't about blending in to Kine society, it encourages urban legends and superstitions to evolve from Kindred action in the shadows; and justice is settled through trial by ordeal.  The atmosphere becomes one of creative and magical flourishing, but extremely volatile. The Domain becomes a Garden of Monsters.
answers.q13: The Sacred Scar (not literal) marks a true Acolyte. True Acolytes rejoice from loss of humanity, treating it as an ascension; they are committed to the ordeal by constantly challenging themselves; and devotedly communal, willing to share their vitae through communal rituals. Cuturally, Crones are built different -- they have the Gaze of the Predator where, in their own minds, they do not behave as if they are in the Masquerade. The true test, however, is how an Acolyte reacts to Frenzy. a True Acolyte views this as a Divine Visitation and ride the wave, observing the Beast and then meditating on what was revealed.
answers.q14: The Final Pruning -- this follows the natural cycle of growth, harvest and rot. If stagnation is death, then when a Kindred has reached their peak, they must be pruned (or even set conditions for themselves to be pruned by others) to allow for fresh growth. The ultimate goal to become the monster is to become a feral force of nature. Final Death becomes the goal in order to achieve the next ascension beyond the Requiem.
answers.q15: reverence becomes savagery at the Total Eclipse of the Self (not of the Heart), this is when an experience moves from something in the mind to something felt. The cross into brutality can occur when the Acolyte transitions from reverence of life to sanctification of pain, where the Acolyte becomes the monster.
answers.q16: Other covenants come back to the Circle because Acolytes are the most honest of the kindred regarding their nature and the Beast. The Sanctum are in a state of denial and secretly covet the authenticity of the Crone, but can never admit this.
answers.q17: My character was drawn to the Circle because he sought to understand (and give meaning to) his transformation that was a positive and inspiring (as opposed to self-loathing like the Lancea).  The Circle makes the Acolyte feel part of the world and nature, rather than as an interloper, curse or mistake. The Circle provides authenticity, endless possibility and true community that isn't defined by hierarchy, transaction or guilt.   No other covenant offers this.
answers.q18: My character follows a blend of the Mother of Monsters (Lilith, feral path) which holds the vampire as a predator and creator of nightmares. Within this tradition, I aim to become an apex predator that breeds horrors for the Kine. But this is tempered by the Urbanist modern context where I see technology, specifically visual media, as an outlet for the Mother to express herself.  Most people see technology as outside nature; I see it as part of a broader world that includes everything that the Kine has created. Horrors can be just as primal and terrifying in a modern city as in the middle of the wilderness.  SO, my path is a mix of the creation/birth mythology of Lilith and chaos/entropy of industry, also inspired by my connection to The Eye in the Moulding Room.
answers.q19: My Sire was a critical mentor and guide for me to become inducted into the ways of the Circle. I originally hated and resented him for my Embrace and over a decade of manipulation of my life, nudging me into worse and worse failures. Over time, through his guidance and teaching, I came to understand it all as a tremendous gift. He granted me rebirth to this higher state of existence in the Requiem.
answers.q20: That depends on the power of the Prince. But most likely it would be silence followed by a commitment to undermine and ultimately depose the Prince -- and practice in secret regardless of the decree.
answers.q21: I would happily debate the Sanctified fool and point out the hypocrisy of his position; but I would to this with compassion and sadness, acknowledging how awful it must be for the Sanctified to live with self-hate. Acolytes will always be free; the Sanctified are slaves to an uncaring god.
answers.q22: The mortal probably becomes part of the ritual... and dies.
answers.q23: Educated and if they refuse to amend their ways, expelled or ritually hunted as one of our special hunts.  They become part of a Cruac ritual one way or another...

answers.q02: The Invictus believe in a strict order and hierarchy. Ruling through a leadership structure and oaths.
answers.q03: The Masquerade protects their power structure, the strict guidelines of maintaining the The Masquerade allows them to operate under the same order, hierarchy and exert their control.To the Invictus, breaking the Masquerade is an attack on the very structure of vampire society they exist to uphold.
answers.q04: As vampires themselves in their very nature are predatory they believe that since not all Kindred are equals the same rules apply to hierarchy and to social structure. Without their rules the believe that things would fall to chaos. Those who hold power are beset to prove they deserve to maintain it.
answers.q05: The Invictus see themselves as the continuation of the Camarilla. They believe they are upholding the lore of aristocratic rule which gives them the authority to act as if they are the true leaders of Kindred Society.
answers.q06: As the Invictus rules on merit based service the idea that members must always be striving to get ahead. So scheming underlings will always seek to replace their superiors, so while they strive to be seen doing well it can be a double edged sword as the tall grass gets cut.
answers.q07: The Invictus are aware that you must keep up with the times of modern society make sure they are not lost to obscurity however they bring the same structured hierarchy that they have constantly maintained.
answers.q08:
Oaths are essential to the making of a full member of the Invictus. They are one of the glues that bind them to the order of the Invictus, usually shared between a liege and a vassal they hold ritual purpose as well as benefits, but also come with an edge as one affects the other.

Oath breakers are treated as traitors to the very core of the Invictus, they are usually brought to the Final Death often at times by members of the Order of St. Martin.
answers.q09: They view them as ways of obtaining influence not just in Kindred society and interfering with their political enemies through various channels.
answers.q10: New members of the Invictus should attach themselves to influential members who can explain the intricacies of social movements and learn the hierarchy.
answers.q11: The Invictus believe themselves the rightful rulers of Kindred Society, the Second Estate as an important allies, not equals but shared hierarchy. The view the Carthians as naive trouble makers, the Circle they view with suspicion and distrust due to their nature.
answers.q12: The Invictus if not in rule of a domain will regroup and pool their resources to centralise their power base to reassert control through politics rather than military rule.
answers.q13: Debt is considered the real power as with Boons they can create leverage that stretches over Clans and Covenants from lowly minions to other powerful Kindred. As they can be called in to otherwise overrule alliances or previous allegiances regardless of beliefs. They believe that Blood gives prestige but not in any tangible powerful way and Titles are names of power but a title without backing is empty.
answers.q14: An Elder that controls the most Boons, favours and sworn oaths are the ones that Invictus obeys, they are often intertwined with the system however their power base only exists as long as the system does,
answers.q15: Feudalism could fail in a dying city because a rigid structure might not allow for adaptability in a sudden crisis. If a ruler who adhered to a very strict chain of command found herself in a sudden war footing and she had surrounded herself with sycophants rather than competent leaders who would know how to carry out a war.
answers.q16: They may sneer at the First Estate in public, but most covenants quietly lean on what the Invictus build: predictable domains, enforceable boons, and a court that makes agreements stick. Without that scaffolding, many of their practices become harder to sustain than they admit.
answers.q17: A mixture of pragmatism, upbringing and intrigue. His sire was the one who slowly introduced him into the world of Kindred Society and saved him during the upheaval of his mortal life.
answers.q18: As of this moment he has now become a Baron of the Invictus of Sydney, with a knight and a lord sworn to him through several oaths. (Can provide all from Kurtis and Conan)
answers.q19:
Rene's Liege; The Marquis Eloius Du Ponte acts as an advisor and teacher to Rene, constantly testing him but pulling the strings behind the scenes to sometimes help, sometimes hinder him but always with a lesson in mind. 

This relationship has taught Rene to approach Invictus politics with a measured eye and constant suspicion of surface appearances. He has learned that power is rarely exercised openly, and that the true game is played in implication, timing, and leverage. Rather than reacting impulsively, Rene observes while waiting to understand who benefits from any given outcome before committing himself. He treats every Boon, every alliance and every conflict as part of a larger pattern.

At the same time, there is an unspoken tension beneath their dynamic. Rene knows he is being shaped into something useful, perhaps even formidable but also something that may ultimately serve his sire’s long-term designs. This awareness does not breed open defiance; instead, it sharpens his ambition. He studies not only the lessons the Marquis intends to teach, but also the methods by which those lessons are delivered. In doing so, Rene is not simply learning how to survive within Invictus hierarchy. In the end the plan is learning how to manipulate it with the same quiet precision, preparing for the night when he is no longer the one being tested, but the one setting the terms.
answers.q20: Rene would take all matters into account before dealing punishment. Most likely a public humiliation to remind them that they are acting on behalf of themselves but also the Invictus as a whole, if you show too much leniency you lose respect and face, if you use too much punishment you can create another potential enemy wasting time scheming against you rather than the real enemy.
answers.q21: Depending on the situation as well, something major would involve a public polite discussion attempting to come to a resolution that resolves the weakness or allows for compensation to save face. If severe and the Prince is unreasonable quiet subversion undermining their power until seizing it.
answers.q22: Rene would make it clear that unless Prestige was mentioned he wouldn't dishonour his standing by demeaning himself. Should that be used as leverage over him then he would then pass on the task to an underling with the knowledge that the Elder put it upon as both and that the underling would be rewarded by Rene should they succeed and protect them (depending on how badly) they failed.
answers.q23:
The Invictus as whole may not feel they owe anything to those outside of the First Estate, they may have the feeling of being duty bound to assist the Second Estate but the rest may not be owed anything but a courtesy of a response. 

Rene would way up their value as a future ally, if it costs him political capital in the short term, but could serve him better as an agent in the long term he would step in an offer protection and a resolution to the matter. I

answers.q02: Power for power's sake. Power takes a long time to build, but is destroyed comparatively quickly, so establishing and adhering to traditions and structures designed to maintain that power must be prioritised. Because as the oldest Covenant, it is the duty of the Invictus to rule and enforce rules for the protection and betterment of all Kindred
answers.q03: It is the tangled web of unspoken protocol, etiquette, and temporal power that Invictus members learn and navigate. Successful movement through and understanding of the conspiracy shows ones experience and commitment to the Invictus cause. Protecting the Masquerade is central because in that protection, the Invictus generate their right to rule over all kindred (some may also say that such closeness to the Masquerade better allows Invictus to get away with breaking it)
answers.q04: They believe in the right of an individual to rule over all those beneath them. That the kindred below should toil for their masters above and that (in theory at least) the masters above should show grace to the kindred below. I believe, deep down, that Invictus believe this to be the natural order because they say it is, and who's going to question the First Estate?
answers.q05: The Invictus would say that they are a direct continuation of the Camarilla, that they were and are the first Covenant. It matters because this is the founding myth of the Invictus. To call it into question calls into question the legitimacy of Invictus as a Covenant and, perhaps more importantly, their right to rule over other Kindred. The enemy's of the Invictus might put forward that the destruction of the Camarilla was the result of a hostile take-over by Invictus, that true Camarilla history has been destroyed, and that what we have now is a web of lies and half-truths manufactured by Invictus designed to create their right to rule.
answers.q06: The tension lies between ones acceptance of this statement, and one's duty to the Covenant. Above all the hierarchy must be maintained, your plotting had better not disrupt the established structure of the Covenant. It means that lieges may need to keep an inconvenient subordinate around, and it means that a subordinate may need to accept the rule of an inept liege.
answers.q07: It reconciles this tension through their strict adherence to hierarchy and their bending new technology to the preservation of The Conspiracy. It doesn't matter whether the ruler wears a crown, a suit, a leather jacket, or a mitre. So long as the ones below are well equipped and doing the work to maintain those above, the Conspiracy will be maintained, and Invictus will hold the keys to temporal power
answers.q08: Oaths serve as a way for a vassal and a liege to make a formal agreement, exchanging services or swearing fealty to each other. What makes them binding (apart from the presumption that both parties benefit from the agreement) is the threat of punishment. The Invictus take the sanctity of an Oath seriously, people caught making a mockery of oaths are often removed from the Estate (sometimes through Final Death)
answers.q09: They use them as vessels for temporal power, and to keep The Conspiracy intact
answers.q10: It is related to the Conspiracy of Silence. One learns the complex etiquette through years of experience and devotion to the Covenant (or perhaps by becoming powerful enough to dictate the etiquette themselves)
answers.q11: It depends whether they are in power or not. If the Invictus are in power, they expect everyone to fall in line under them. If they are not, they assimilate and show deference just long enough for an Invictus member to regain the reigns of control. The First and Second Estates have a long standing alliance that stretches back to the Dark/Heroic ages of mankind, where church and court ruled all of Europe with unquestionable authority, where Invictus rule, you will often find the Lancea et Sanctum
answers.q12: Most would rather capitulate than face a final death, assimilating into the power base of the incoming Covenant and within their Inner Circle, plotting the installation of a rightful Invictus ruler once more. In the mean time they will train their subordinates on the value of duty and deference to their better, in this way preparing an areas Kindred for eventual Invictus rule
answers.q13: Debt does. Blood and Title serve as useful alibis for the holding of power; they are the trappings that justify its existence. But the only thing that represents power in its raw form is what others owe you, what leverage you have to get others to do what you want them to, rather than what they would like to do
answers.q14: The Invictus elder everyone obeys is gracious to the loyal, and iron-fisted to the insubordinate. They adhere to the letter of tradition, and that earns them great influence amongst the Invictus. But I happen to know that they arrived in this demesne under an Oath of the Refugee, and that there are those out there that still seek to bring them to a final death. Information about the elders location could be very valuable, but I will not speak of it, because my elder is my better, and regardless of my own machinations I will not be the one caught pushing over the pedestal they are on
answers.q15: The real world church (and more recently the American government) often has to run cover for pedophiles in order to maintain the legitimacy of their hierarchy. When a hierarchy is upheld primarily for the sake of its upholding, it allows true corruption to fester until eventually, no amount of effort can contain it and the structure fails catastrophically
answers.q16:
The Second Estate. They might say that our hierarchy is a reflection of the Divine, but the Divine don't rule here, and we do not cast reflections (at least for the Kine). Take away the structure that we have ingrained into the minds of our subjects and suddenly they must reckon with the idea that their guiding father Longinus was just another Vampire, not a divine figurehead.

The Second Estate would devolve into both physical AND spiritual chaos
answers.q17: Ambition is the closest word but perhaps Desire is better. It was a taste of power that infatuated them, and they seek more of it. They are young (both in Kine and Kindred years) and see themselves as indestructible with their new power
answers.q18: I seek to legitimately claim the seat of my foster-sire, I will use my time in Sydney to prove that I am capable of acting independently for the good of him, and the First Estate as a whole. I currently hold little station, though I aspire to become a Knight for my foster-sire until the day I am capable of succeeding him
answers.q19: My first experience with an Invictus member was my formative one, and it was with my foster-sire. I was enjoying the feeling of letting the Beast loose on a kine and let it go too far. I killed them, violently. It wasn't long before I was found by my foster-sire and given a crash course on why one doesn't poach within his demesne. I saw in him the true power of the beast, not it's wild, rabid physicality, but the true power it is capable of when tempered with grace and control. I don't know what he saw in me, but through his grace I was given a chance to serve him, and since then I have never looked back
answers.q20:
I would base my decision on what decision I believe my foster-sire would make. He would likely choose punishment, which again brings into question why he showed leniency to me?

Regardless, it would set a precedent of none being above the sanctity of oaths or the breaking of Invictus code as a whole. It sends a message to the other Covenants that our own are not above our laws, so perhaps they should tread even more carefully. But it also exposes a weakness, it displays an inflexibility that could be exploited by our enemies (what happens when someone of station is to be judged over crimes fabricated by out opposition?)
answers.q21: It depends my evaluation of the First Estates current power base in the area. If I believe the seat is ripe, Open opposition is the most direct path to power. If we lack the influence to make a bid for power, perhaps I put my weight behind a suitable candidate (who also happens to be inept, and now they also owe me a favour). And sometimes you need to simply know when the means to power are outside of your reach, and bide your time
answers.q22: If it were my foster-sire, or refusing would bring shame on him, I would accept. I would do so in the hope that through my loyalty to my benefactor, I would earn his protection against the consequences should my actions be discovered. If the order came from a superior who wasn't my liege I would expect an explanation as to how my actions fit into the bigger picture, and ensure that I receive recognition or reward for my sacrifice. If it was something I felt particularly strong about, I would likely ignore leadership - For example if I felt particularly wronged by a rival vampire and my plot for revenge was uncovered and discouraged by an elder, I would likely still pursue revenge - though I would not hide that fact, I would say to my betters face that words wont stop me
answers.q23: I would find a Notary willing to bind my ally and I in an Oath of the Handshake Deal. I would offer my assistance to them, and in turn I would be able to demand a favour of them commensurate to my actions for them. Should they refuse when the time comes, I will claim something that more than makes up for my political loses. This oath would bind us for a year and a day (though I'm sure to find a use for it before then)

answers.q02: The Lance are the Capital G God worshipping faction of the Requiem setting, who’s purpose is to guide folks to the Worship of God through tormenting folks to test their faith.
answers.q03: Longinus was the centurion that speared Jesus, got his blood splashed in his mouth, and was thus damned as a Vampire by God. He is the Progenitor of the Lance faith. The Third Version of Judas means (basically) that there is no place in heaven for the Damned, but that they should also no abandon their faith because of it.
answers.q04: It is divine purpose. We are God’s chosen monsters.
answers.q05: It means that to be “Sanctified” one must maintain and actively practice the faith of God, seeking no blessing or forgiveness, accepting that Damnation IS our purpose.
answers.q06: Sanctified worship is the manifestation of principle that Light cannot exist without Darkness. That the purpose of a Vampire is to test the faith of those who would claim to be Christian. They cannot worship alongside mortals, because their purpose is to guide them through fear. They fiercely protect their flocks because they believe they are building and supporting a strong congregation. In a twisted way, they believe that while it is their role to teach through fear, it is also to protect them as a kind of reward for their faith, but also to show them what could happen if they were to abandon it.
answers.q07: They are shepherds. They test the flock through exploiting weakness of faith and “moral” character and mortal fears, to push them back to the teachings of the Church, thus creating and maintaining a strong faithful body in reverence to God and his teachings.
answers.q08: It is the “Vampire Bible”, with all the loaded bullshit that comes with it. From a character perspective, Carver is of the Monarchal faction, however open to debate of the Principals of the Faith, recognising that robust discussion and debate builds a stronger Damned.
answers.q09: Theban Sorcery are the Dark Miracles of God. Cruac is blood magic and heresy, masquerading as the favour of their “Gods”, Theban is the divine manifestation of the Faithful Damned.
answers.q10: The Sanctified collect, collate and store knowledge of all faiths. Those that support and prove the existence of their doctrine are studied and shared (the bits they deem ‘safe’ to do so) and the rest are either suppressed by locking away, or destroyed if deemed to be too dangerous.
answers.q11: The First Estate (ie the Invictus) are the Temporal Leaders of Kindred Society, propped up and supported by the Sanctified since the times of Rome. The relationship between the First and Second Estates is essential for a civilised Kindred society. Carthians are a movement of free thinkers and “enlightenment”, destabilising the established status quo. The Crone are the Heathen. At best, misguided, at worst, Heretical. They are not to be trusted.
answers.q12: They close ranks, finding ways to destabilise and weaken those Kindred (or others) who persecute the Sanctified, while simultaneously consolidating their mortal Flocks to maintain a stable power base from which to launch their counter-offensive.
answers.q14: Those that demonstrate Blind obedience to the Faith are the most terrifying Damned to Carver. In that path, complacency and heresy take root, and Heretical Cults spawn. We must be forever vigilant, viewing the world through the eyes of God’s Chosen Monster.
answers.q15: The Crusades. They were about expansion and greed, using Faith as the excuse to “reclaim” the Holy Land.
answers.q16: For all their rhetoric, most of the Circle of the Crone acknowledge the existence of God. They must. And without the Sanctified, they would have no “Oppressor” to rally against. The Sanctified are the Crone’s greatest recruitment tool.
answers.q17: He was given to the Lance as was part of the Tradition of his Lineage. Most return to the First Estate, some do not. Carver didn’t.
answers.q18: Carver is the Covenant’s “Fixer”. He is not Clergy. He manages Mortal ties and provides resources.
answers.q19: Carver acknowledges that while the current Bishop’s Creed does not strictly adhere to with his traditional Monachal beliefs, that they are still a capable Sanctified, and that while other Creeds exist, we all serve the same God. All roads lead to the Sanctified.
answers.q20: It is delusion. They require education. In only the way that the Sanctified can. *evil grin*
answers.q21: Carver would still say his prayers in secret, and with other like minded Sanctified. Faith is Faith, and not strong if you would let the State suppress your connection to it.
answers.q22: Refuse to dignify the heresy with a response. They are looking to pick a fight, not have a debate. Observation of circumstances change the outcome. Carver would find them later to offer a true, personal, private debate if the true goal is to speak about theology, and not grandstand.
answers.q23: Carver would try to save them. Murder is denial of God’s judgement and it is not our place to deny him that. If possible, the Bestial Damned would be capture, contained and “put on ice” until such time as they could be awakened and reasoned with.

answers.q02: The Lancea et Sanctum is formed around the teachings of the Kindred prophet Longinus and the idea that vampires serve the sacred role of 'God's perfect monsters'. The Lance watches over the Kine, providing tests for the faithful to either break or reinforce their convictions, unseen protection for God's children, and moral counsel for other Kindred.
answers.q03: Longinus was a Roman legionnaire who pierced Christ with a spear (the 'lance' of the Lancea et Sanctum) during the crucifixion on Golgotha. He was a prophet, cursed by God to fulfil a divine purpose, and historically one of the few vampires to be cursed without receiving the Embrace. He is one of the authors of the Testament of Longinus, the texts upon which the Lancea et Sanctum is based.
answers.q04: It is a divine purpose. Those who are cursed with vampirism are already bound for perdition but may serve part of their sentence upon the Earth. God seeks only to make vampires out of people who were sinful in life and would never have been able to reach heaven in the first place.
answers.q05: A 'faith of praxis' is the idea that the Damned can express their faith through actions. The Damned find salvation by bringing mortals into God's light. Although they may be already dead and destined to go to Hell, they can at least use their remaining nights on Earth to help guide the truly worthy to the kingdom of heaven.
answers.q06: Longinus was the Roman centurion who pierced Jesus's side during the crucifixion in the New Testament. Drinking the blood of Christ cursed him with vampirism, so Longinus's power as a kindred prophet is directly tied to Christ's divinity. The Sanctified do not worship alongside mortal Christians because they believe themselves to be cursed by God and incapable receiving forgiveness for their sins. The Damned are not human and are therefore not made in God's image, meaning they do not receive God's grace and do not benefit from the same worship people engage in.
answers.q07: The Sanctified offer temptation to mortal folk, giving them an opportunity to stray from the path of righteousness. If the faithful fall to this temptation then they were never worthy of entering the kingdom of heaven, whereas those who are capable of resisting are shown to be strong in conviction. It ties heavily into the Christian doctrine of God gifting 'free will' to mankind so that their truest moral character may be tested.
answers.q08: It is a series of books dictated by the Roman legionnaire Longinus and written by the vampire Monachus detailing the miracle of Longinus's transformation and the divine role imparted unto him and all vampires by God. It details his life, the nature of vampirism, and rules for Kindred to live by. It is the text upon which the Lancea et Sanctum is founded.
answers.q09: Theban Sorcery was a gift from an angel to a member of the Lancea et Sanctum while travelling with Christian Soldiers from Thebes to Gaul. Theban Sorcery is a form of vampiric miracles and can only be performed by Kindred who are in touch with their humanity. Cruac, on the other hand, does not draw its power from God but instead from the Beast within and correlates with a lack of humanity.
answers.q10: The Lancea et Sanctum maintains libraries and archives of texts pertinent to kindred folk. They destroy knowledge that they deem dangerous, such as knowledge of the supernatural, occult or profane, or heretical, especially if it contradicts the Testament of Longinus.
answers.q11: The Lancea et Sanctum typically regards the Carthian Movement neutrally, admiring their conviction even if it is entirely misplaced. The Circle of the Crone is the Lance's arch-nemesis, as they worship pagan deities, devils and demons, completely antithetical to their monotheism. The First and Second Estate are closely tied as it mirrors the traditional relationship between state and church. The Lancea et Sanctum often lends legitimacy to the Invictus, providing an endorsement similar to the 'divine right of kings'.
answers.q12: The Lancea et Sanctum perceives its own faith as a shield and will do everything they can to weather a storm, just as other religions do when faced with persecution. Some members of the Lance are more militant and will not hesitate to wage holy war or crusade against their foes.
answers.q13: Diablerie. Passing judgement over fellow Kindred and granting them Final Death alone is a sin, but to cannibalise them in body and spirit as well places a permanent stain on the soul of any Kindred. Any diablerist should be immediately staked and imprisoned until Judgement Day.
answers.q14: Conrad fears an authority that craves power for its own sake and uses the teachings of Longinus as a convenient facade. To him, true authority belongs to God and any Kindred who rules without humility or due deference to Longinus's sacred texts is unworthy.
answers.q15: Conrad has seen his covenant effectively grant Final Death to members of his Covenant, a judgement that is reserved for God and God alone. A falsely repentant Sanctified fleeing crimes in another city was apprehended by the mercenary and bounty hunter Charlie Ballsack, and the Bishop gave up this Sanctified, practically giving him the death sentence. Conrad saw this as a betrayal of the creed, that the Church leaders would sacrifice their principles for convenience.
answers.q16: The Invictus and the Lance require one another because the Invictus can often lose their humanity when dealing with power in the cold, calculating manner that often befits the most successful amongst their ranks. The Church's steadfast dedication to spirituality and a purpose beyond survival reminds Kindred to cling to the last vestiges of their personhood so that they might preserve their undeath for more nights to come. Additionally, the Invictus are often more willing to make the hard decisions for the city that the Lance may be more reticent to enact - one takes action, the other provides counsel.
answers.q17: Conrad was a corrupt police detective in life and had an interest in the occult. His Sire made him reflect on his sinful behaviour and proved the existence of God beyond the shadow of a doubt through Theban Sorcery. Conrad was converted after his Embrace and looks back on his mortal life with disdain.
answers.q18: Conrad is an inquisitor, meaning he has the duty of testing his fellow Sanctifieds' faith. While his rank does not grant him immediate authority and status, it does keep him close to the Bishop and is effectively makes him a glorified tattle tale. Conrad is beholden to the Bishop at all times and can only act in accordance with their commands.
answers.q19: A former Crone named Carver was brought to the Lance as a sorcerous eunuch to pay for crimes committed using Cruac. Conrad saw that Carver was truly repentant and helped with his rehabilitation. Although Conrad is an inquisitor and his job requires him to root out heretics, he is not above forgiveness and believes sincerely in the redemptive power of the Lance.
answers.q20: Blasphemy. God does not forgive the Damned, therefore the Damned in question is a liar and a blasphemer. As an inquisitor, Conrad would report this heresy to the Bishop immediately and then await further instruction from them before proceeding. The Bishop should either offer an opportunity for the heretic to repent their wicked ways and disavow their prior blasphemous statements or expel them from the Lancea et Sanctum if they refuse to do so. Either way, Conrad should maintain a watchful eye over this individual, as this one instance of heresy may be indicative of further disobedience down the line.
answers.q21: Secular authority should never dare to interfere in the church's private matters. The first step would be softly reminding the Praxis of the Lancea et Sanctum's role, their importance moral bastion for all kindred and the foolishness of antagonising blood sorcerers, offering an opportunity for them to correct their mistake. If their humble plea is disregarded then Conrad would maintain an outwardly docile appearance but defy the Praxis and continue to practise in secret, as Roman Christians did under the rule of Emperor Nero. He would work with other members of his covenant to secretly undermine the Praxis and replace them with someone more amenable to the Sanctified. Conrad will only recognise the legitimacy of the state when it coexists harmoniously with the church.
answers.q22: Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live.' The Circle are never interested in serious, dignified debate and will always engage in deceit to spread their heretical lies. Their intention is to degrade, debase and humiliate the institution of the church, so we must not allow them to exploit our pride to draw us into their rhetorical traps. Conrad would refuse to engage with the witch, brushing their provocations off with tranquility, never allowing these devils to shake his faith.
answers.q23: A Sanctified may never grant Final Death to another Damned, as that is strictly forbidden by Longinus's teachings. However, they may not be saved, as God has deemed them unfit to fulfil their purpose. Therefore they must be staked, hidden, and locked away, never to walk the Earth again.

answers.q02: The Lancea et Sanctum is a “big-tent” religion, with many denominations and interpretations of their two scriptures: the ‘Sanguineous Catechism’ or the ‘Testament of Longinus’. This ranges from bastardised Christianity (mostly), bastardised Islam and bastardised Judaism and even a little bastardised agnosticism; all the Abrahamic faiths plus some. The stated purpose is to be the moral authority of the “Sanctified” or “Damned”, given Kindred a moral/ethical framework and divine purpose to their existence. The alternate purpose, broadly, is to drive mortals to the monotheistic god of the Abrahamic religions. Their hierophants the sticks to salvation’s carrot; sheepdogs biting at the heels of the flock of the Lamb to keep them in line.
answers.q03: He was the soldier, a right evil bastard of a soldier too, who stabbed Jesus with a spear, and then when the blood of Christ anointed him by falling into his mouth he became the first divinely appointed Kindred. As miraculous as the virgin birth, this Immaculate Conception (or Maculate Conception? Because inverted unholiness/holiness?) caused a revival amount the Kindred of the day, and stocked a movement that would usurp the Camarilla of Rome. Betrayal as the ultimate sacrifice casts Longinus as a repentant Third Form of Judas figure, the acting as the hand of God. Judas or Longinus did not just betray Jesus for greed or wanton defiance of God; he surrendered his reputation and soul to eternal damnation to fulfill the divine plan of salvation. Longinus is a Dark Messiah, whose damnation was divinely inspired and ordained as a true Messianic sacrifice.
answers.q04: All three, in some ways. The Sanctified/Damned zealously bear the weight of damnation as they bear the cross of their monstrous nature, the Beast, knowing that they are part of God's Great Plan, but they have not been immediately condemned to Hell like the Devil, as surely is their due. They see the Glory of Heaven as the ultimate carrot for humanity's salvation and themselves as the ultimate stick. If a Kindred lives to the Last Days however, they may be *theoretically* granted clemency and get to go to Heaven. So they are cursed by the sin of their undead existence, but are blessed with a commuted sentence to damnation, as long as they properly execute their Divine Purpose.
answers.q05: Faith of praxis refers to the integration of belief with action. Kindred of the Second Estate’s religious faith is not just a set of doctrines but is lived out through concrete, ethical, and transformative actions. The Damned find salvation through their part in their view of God’s plan. They are the labourers in the vineyard. The workers of the field, ripe with God’s harvest, His kine. They drive the mortals in their care to a Heaven they almost certainly can never enter.
answers.q06: The Kindred usually have no use for the ethics for Modern Christianity. Salvation is lost to them. The kine are not brothers and sisters in God’s love, they are prey and chattel, but prey and chattel to safeguard for a Master who is returning. God will come back, and if the Kindred have not cared for their flock, they will be worse off than the Wicked and Lazy servant in The Parable of the Talents (or Bags of Gold) in Matthew 25:14-30.
answers.q07: Through pin-point persecution, whittling away heretics and con-men. Thus the Kindred of the Lancea et Sanctum conclude that they are to serve as object lessons to humanity; they are the price of sin. By preying on mortals they remind them that life is fleeting and nothing compares to the Heaven the worthy attain. To this end, the Sanctified zealously bear the weight of damnation as they revel in their monstrous nature, knowing that they are part of God's Great Plan. They put the fear of God into the congregation. Literally.
answers.q08: Longinus was a colossal cunt. Dude who did every sin you can imagine, and then at least one or two you probably wouldn’t imagine if given a week to work out the worst shit you could. He also, became a soldier, and just so happened to be the one who stabbed a random Jewish carpenter and gained undying super-powers, and became the first Kindred prophet. He brought the word that Kindred/the sanctified/the damned were given purpose by God, cause an angel told him so (very John Smith of him). His life prior to his divinely appointed immaculate Embrace is a parable proving that sin has its punishment. His wanderings after teach how to live as a repentant Damned. All of this was written down in the Black Abbey nearly 5 hundred years after the death of Christ by this poor monk named Monacis, and that became the Testement of Longinus.
answers.q09: It’s cool blood Magic, with a flagellant theme. The Damned/Sanctified discovered it in Thebes, in caves underground with a bunch of magical writing (shown the way by an angel, supposedly). These powers require Vitae to use, like other blood sorceries, some devotions and disciplines, but are best utilised by those with high Humanity; the opposite applying to Crúac sorcery. My character wouldn’t know too much about to, seeing as he’s a Sorcerous Eunuch, because apparently you can geld someone’s ability to use this power by torturing them for a month.
answers.q10: It uses the knowledge tactically. Heresies, like the Livian Heresy (justice for Livia OOC, poor thing didn’t deserve that) or the Crimson Cavalry (KKK but somehow even worse) are destroyed, and are deemed heresies and invalidated by the detailed records of religious and apocryphal texts kept by the Lance. It destroys or suppresses anything which challenges its narratives, and preserves those which do. When you’re in a society of immortal beings, being the people who’s job it is to write things down makes you pretty important, as this _will_ come up later.
answers.q11:
The First and Second estate get along swimmingly. The Invictus are the manipulators Worldly, secular sides of life, and the Lance are the orchestrators of the religious and spiritual. Their paths rarely cross, and they can ammicably disagree on fundamentals, while still having identical goals and aspirations. 

Carthians are another matter. While mostly secular, they have some ideas about the dictates of morality which the Lance don’t gel with. It feels similar to the Enlightenment philosophy and the church’s theology and how they are at odds. While _technically_ seperate and even complementary at times, they often try to explain the same phenomena and come up with different answers, and so, naturally, one has to be wrong. And when that happens between two dogmatic ideologues… tensions get heated quick.

The Crones are barefaced naturalistic pagans, and they and the Lance HATE each other. Cats and dogs type shit. They elevate the divine feminine, the Beast and the naturalist explanation of how kindred came to be; while the Lance elevates the divine masculine, the Human and preached the seperation of the natural and the spiritual. They couldn’t be more different if they tried.
answers.q12: Abrahamic faiths _love_ persecution, and the Sanctified are no different. It validates the martyrdom prophecies/victim complex baked into their theology, and justifies and rationalises their existence. When the covenant is persecuted, they are legitimised. They grow and bounce back stronger, with more radical converts. They overcome the adversity with the knowledge that adversity proves they are on the path of righteousness, allowing their suffering and martyrdom to inflame others to their cause.
answers.q13: To let the Beast corrupt and control the Human. To lose your grip of yourself, and to become the monster that kills and destroys without piety or mercy. All vampires suffer their Beast, but to let it control you is seen as shameful and indecent. Many rituals within the faith revolve around provoking the Beast and then overcoming it with the Human, through piety, prayer and faith (or, more correctly, using these as tools to leverage sheer, bloody Willpower.) Gangrel, like Kirk, have a _real_ tough time with this, as their Bane is a rampant Beast. While this is a known fact among Kindred, and some tolerate the Savages for their gifts, they are almost never fully trusted.
answers.q14:
My character _despises_ himself. He sees the sins he committed as equal or greater than the sins committed by Longinus, and knows without hesitation of his damnation. He doesn’t frighten easy, due to this, as any punishment he receives is just and fair. 

But what terrifies him the most is the condemnation of authority in letting his Beast control him. He is ashamed of the strength of the monster within, and hates that he cannot hold it at bay. Being ostracised by the faith would emotionally break him.
answers.q15: Heresies would be a great example. The most striking of which (the one I chose for my character to be Embraced in) would be the Crimson Cavalry. They hated Theban Sorcery, thinking it witchcraft, and became an apocalyptic cult, yearning for the end-times. Right evil bastards.
answers.q16: The Carthians need the Lance, as they need to demonstrate their dedication to democracy. They legitimise their tolerance. If they were to persecute the Lance, they’d be no better than the Invictus. The Carthians also have little-to-no spirituality in their doctrines and laws. While they can dictate reality to some extent, the fact that there are actual ghosts, demons and monsters out there proves, to some extent, that a secular understanding of reality isn’t the only rational one. The Lance provides both a case to practice tolerating those who are different than you, and to be a legitimate alternative for a differing, valid opinion.
answers.q17: He was faithful in life, to the point of hating one denomination of Christianity over another so passionately that violence and hatred seemed natural and necessary. He was a devout Protestant Anglican, and continued to be initially after his Embrace, as he wasn’t told he was Damned, or nearly anything if the Dance Macabre. He was told his enemies were Catholic cultists of a Dark Messiah who raped his own mother and committed blood sorcery from a twisted heresy, and he hunted them and their servants like the good little Christian he was. When he realised he was Damned, that his first-love faith was lost to him and that he would never see Heaven, it was all far, far too late. He despises his naive arrogance and his ignorance, and seeks to atone in his cursed half-life for all his sins and failures; the only alleviation from his guilt being dogged servitude to the Lance.
